section.title: The LORD Will Restore His People
section.sectionRationale: Following the coming King, God turns to His scattered flock. He condemns the false shepherds and worthless idols that left the people leaderless, then promises to raise up true leadership from Judah, the cornerstone and tent peg, and to strengthen, gather, and bring home His people from every land. The good Shepherd will replace the bad ones and restore His flock.
section.thesis: Because idols and false shepherds have left God's people scattered and oppressed, the LORD Himself will visit His flock, raise up true leadership from Judah, and strengthen, redeem, and gather His people home, so that they walk in His name.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the folly of idols and diviners that leave the flock shepherdless; God's anger at the false shepherds and His raising of the cornerstone and true leaders from Judah; and His promise to strengthen, whistle for, redeem, and gather His people from Egypt and Assyria to walk in His name.

verses.text: Ask the LORD for rain in springtime; the LORD makes the storm clouds, and He will give everyone showers of rain and crops in the field.
For idols speak deceit and diviners see illusions; they tell false dreams and offer empty comfort. Therefore the people wander like sheep, oppressed for lack of a {{roeh|shepherd}}.
“My anger burns against the shepherds, and I will punish the leaders. For the LORD of Hosts attends to His flock, the house of Judah; He will make them like His royal steed in battle.
The {{pinnah|cornerstone}} will come from Judah, the {{yated|tent peg}} from him, as well as the battle bow and every ruler together.
They will be like mighty men in battle, trampling the enemy in the mire of the streets. They will fight because the LORD is with them, and they will put the horsemen to shame.

units.label: Gathered and brought home
units.note: redeemed to walk in His name

verses.text: I will strengthen the house of Judah and save the house of Joseph. I will restore them because I have compassion on them, and they will be as though I had not rejected them. For I am the LORD their God, and I will answer them.
Ephraim will be like a mighty man, and their hearts will be glad as with wine. Their children will see it and be joyful; their hearts will rejoice in the LORD.
I will whistle for them to gather, for I have {{padah|redeemed}} them; and they will be as numerous as they once were.
Though I sow them among the nations, they will remember Me in distant lands; they and their children will live and return.
I will bring them back from Egypt and gather them from Assyria. I will bring them to Gilead and Lebanon until no more room is found for them.
They will pass through the sea of distress and strike the waves of the sea; all the depths of the Nile will dry up. The pride of Assyria will be brought down, and the scepter of Egypt will depart.
I will strengthen them in the LORD, and in His name they will walk,” declares the LORD.

roeh.gloss: A shepherd, one who leads, feeds, and protects the flock; in Scripture a standard image for kings and leaders, and for God Himself. The people wander oppressed for lack of a true shepherd, exposed to false leaders and empty idols. The whole passage moves toward the LORD taking up the shepherd's role for His flock, fulfilled in Christ the good Shepherd.

pinnah.gloss: A cornerstone, the foundational stone that anchors and aligns a whole building. Promised to come from Judah, it pictures the stable, God-given leadership that will replace the false shepherds. The image joins the wider biblical theme of the cornerstone, fulfilled in Christ, the sure foundation and chief cornerstone on whom God's people are built.

yated.gloss: A tent peg or the peg driven into a wall to hang weight on. Like the cornerstone, it is an image of firm, dependable support; from Judah will come the peg on which the community's hopes securely hang. Isaiah uses the same image of a peg in a sure place for a faithful leader, pointing beyond human rulers to the reliable One God provides.

padah.gloss: To redeem, to ransom, to secure release by paying a price. God whistles for His scattered people to gather because He has redeemed them; their return rests on His prior act of ransom, not their own effort. It echoes the redemption from Egypt and looks ahead to the greater redemption accomplished by Christ, who ransoms His people from every land and from sin itself.

basic.context: After the coming King, God addresses His scattered flock and the leaders who failed them. The people had turned to idols and diviners for guidance, which gave only empty comfort, so they wandered like sheep without a shepherd. God's anger burns against these false shepherds. He promises to visit His flock and raise up true leadership from Judah, the cornerstone and the tent peg. Then He pledges to strengthen, whistle for, redeem, and gather His people from Egypt, Assyria, and every land, bringing them home to walk in His name.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic promise using pastoral and military imagery. The contrast is between worthless idols and false shepherds on one side and the LORD as true Shepherd on the other. The cornerstone and tent peg from Judah point to God-given leadership fulfilled in the Messiah. The gathering from Egypt and Assyria pictures a new exodus, a homecoming of God's people that reaches beyond the return from exile.
basic.originalLanguages: The flock wanders for lack of a roeh, a shepherd. God promises leadership from Judah, the pinnah, cornerstone, and the yated, tent peg, images of firm support. And He gathers His people because He has padah, redeemed, ransomed them, so their homecoming rests on His prior act of redemption.
basic.typology: The LORD who shepherds His scattered flock points to Christ, the good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep. The cornerstone from Judah is fulfilled in Christ, the chief cornerstone. The redeemed people brought home in a new exodus foreshadow the ingathering Christ accomplishes, gathering His scattered children into one.
basic.christ: The empty idols and false shepherds leave the sheep without care, which sets up the promise fulfilled in Jesus. He is the cornerstone from Judah, the sure foundation, and He is the good Shepherd who seeks the lost, heals the broken, and lays down His life for the flock. The redemption on which the homecoming rests is accomplished in His blood, by which He gathers His people from every nation.
basic.god: God is angry at leaders who exploit and mislead His people, and tender toward the flock they neglected. He takes personal responsibility for His sheep, promising to strengthen, redeem, and gather them out of compassion, treating them as though He had never rejected them. He is a redeeming God who brings His people home and enables them to walk in His name.
basic.crossReferences: Ezekiel 34 condemns false shepherds and promises God as Shepherd. John 10 reveals Jesus as the good Shepherd. Psalm 118 and 1 Peter 2 present the cornerstone. Isaiah 11 foretells a second exodus gathering God's people from Assyria and Egypt.
basic.oneStory: God will not leave His flock to idols and false shepherds; He raises the cornerstone from Judah and gathers His redeemed people home in a new exodus. This points to Christ, the good Shepherd and chief cornerstone, who ransoms and gathers His scattered people, and the story runs on toward the one flock brought home to walk forever in His name.

context.literary: The continuation of the first burden, following the coming King of chapter 9 and preparing for the rejected shepherd of chapter 11. The shepherd theme dominates this stretch of the book: false shepherds condemned here, the good shepherd rejected in chapter 11, and the struck shepherd of chapter 13, all part of Zechariah's sustained meditation on leadership fulfilled in Christ.
context.historical: The returned community still felt scattered, with many Israelites remaining dispersed across the old empires of Egypt and Assyria, that is the wider lands of exile. Tempted like their ancestors to seek guidance from idols and diviners, they needed the assurance that the LORD Himself, not empty images or failed leaders, would gather and restore them.
context.canonical: The passage joins Ezekiel 34 and Jeremiah 23 in condemning false shepherds and promising God's own shepherding, fulfilled in John 10. The cornerstone from Judah ties into the cornerstone theme of Psalm 118, Isaiah 28, and 1 Peter 2, and the gathering from Egypt and Assyria echoes the second-exodus hope of Isaiah 11 and Hosea 11.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the emptiness of idols and false shepherds, to assure God's scattered people that the LORD Himself will care for His flock, raise up true leadership from Judah, and redeem and gather them home from every land to walk in His name.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The condemnation of idols and the promises of restoration are revelation, not commands, though verse 1 exhorts the people to ask the LORD, not idols, for rain. The abiding truths are the folly of trusting idols, God's shepherd-care for His people, and redemption as the basis of homecoming.

debates.question: Who are the cornerstone, tent peg, and battle bow from Judah?

views.label: God-given leaders raised up for the flock
views.case: The images name strong, dependable leadership emerging from Judah to replace the false shepherds, restoring the community with firm and faithful rule.

views.label: A messianic cluster fulfilled in Christ
views.case: Cornerstone, peg, and bow converge on one figure, the Messiah from Judah, the sure foundation and mighty deliverer, so the images ultimately point to Christ.

debates.bearing: Either way the promise is of true, God-given leadership from Judah, whose fullest fulfillment is Christ the cornerstone and good Shepherd.

debates.question: What is the gathering from Egypt and Assyria?

views.label: The return of the dispersed from the old lands of exile
views.case: Egypt and Assyria stand for all the places God's people were scattered, and the promise is a literal homecoming of the dispersed to the land.

views.label: A new exodus fulfilled in Christ's ingathering
views.case: The language of passing through the sea and drying up the Nile evokes a second exodus, fulfilled as Christ gathers His scattered people from every nation into one flock.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm God's certain gathering of His redeemed people home, climaxing in Christ's ingathering of His flock from all the earth.

study.originalLanguages: The passage runs on four key words. The flock wanders oppressed for lack of a roeh, a shepherd, exposed to lying idols and failed leaders, and the whole oracle moves toward the LORD taking up the shepherd's role Himself. From Judah He promises the pinnah, the cornerstone that anchors and aligns the whole building, and the yated, the tent peg or wall peg on which weight securely hangs, both images of firm, God-given support to replace the false shepherds, and both echoed by Isaiah of a faithful leader fastened like a peg in a sure place. And the ground of the homecoming is that God has padah, redeemed, ransomed His people; He whistles for them to gather not because they have earned return but because He has already paid to set them free, an echo of the exodus and a foreshadowing of the redemption accomplished in Christ.

typology.type: The LORD shepherding His scattered flock
typology.fulfillment: Jesus the good Shepherd who seeks the lost and lays down His life for the sheep, John 10:11-16 and Ezekiel 34:11-16.
Christ, the chief cornerstone and sure foundation of God's people, Psalm 118:22 and 1 Peter 2:6-7.

typology.type: The redeemed gathered home in a new exodus
typology.fulfillment: Christ gathering His scattered children into one flock from every nation, John 11:52 and Isaiah 11:11-12.

study.christ: This oracle sets up its fulfillment in Jesus by first showing the ruin that false shepherds and empty idols leave behind: sheep wandering with no one to care for them. That is exactly the scene Jesus steps into when He sees the crowds harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd, and has compassion on them. He is the good Shepherd promised here, who does not exploit the flock but lays down His life for it, who seeks the lost and heals the broken. He is the cornerstone from Judah, the stone the builders rejected made the chief cornerstone, the sure foundation on whom God's people are built and aligned. And the whole promise of a redeemed people gathered home from every land is accomplished in Him, for He died to gather into one the scattered children of God, ransoming His flock from every nation so that they might live and walk in His name. The failure of every false shepherd only makes the true Shepherd shine brighter.
study.god: The God revealed here is fiercely protective of His flock and severe toward those who abuse it. His anger burns against the shepherds and leaders who fed themselves and left the sheep to wander after idols and lies. But His heart toward the flock is all compassion: He attends to them, strengthens them, redeems them, and gathers them home, treating them as though He had never rejected them, and He answers them when they call. He is a redeeming God whose gathering of His people rests on His own prior act of ransom, and who is committed not merely to bring them back to a place but to enable them to walk in His name. Judgment on false shepherds and tender restoration of the flock are two sides of the same shepherd-love.

crossReferences.note: God condemns the false shepherds and promises to shepherd His flock Himself, the backdrop to this oracle.
Jesus is the good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep and gathers one flock, fulfilling the shepherd promise.
Christ is the chosen and precious cornerstone, fulfilling the cornerstone from Judah.
Jesus died to gather into one the scattered children of God, fulfilling the gathering of the redeemed.

study.oneStory: God refuses to abandon His flock to idols and false shepherds; He raises the cornerstone from Judah and gathers His redeemed people home in a new exodus. This points to Christ, the good Shepherd and chief cornerstone, who ransoms and gathers His scattered people from every land, and the story moves toward one flock brought home to walk forever in the name of the LORD.

quiz.q: Why do the people wander like sheep?

options.text: They lack a true shepherd and have turned to idols and diviners for empty comfort.
options.note: Verse 2, oppressed for lack of a shepherd, misled by idols and false dreams.

options.text: They have too many good shepherds to follow.
options.note: The problem is the absence of a true shepherd, not too many.

options.text: They refused all rain and food from God.
options.note: Verse 1 invites them to ask God for rain; the core issue is false guidance.

options.text: For no reason the passage gives.
options.note: The text names idols and lack of a shepherd as the reason.

quiz.q: What does God promise will come from Judah?

options.text: The cornerstone, the tent peg, and true leadership for the flock.
options.note: Verse 4 names the cornerstone and tent peg from Judah.

options.text: More idols and diviners.
options.note: God replaces idols with firm, true leadership, not more idols.

options.text: A foreign king to rule them.
options.note: The leadership comes from Judah, not from foreign powers.

options.text: Nothing at all.
options.note: God promises the cornerstone and true leaders from Judah.

quiz.q: On what basis does God gather His scattered people?

options.text: Because He has redeemed them, having ransomed them Himself.
options.note: Verse 8, I will whistle for them to gather, for I have redeemed them.

options.text: Because they earned their return by good works.
options.note: The gathering rests on God's redemption, not their merit.

options.text: Because the nations released them freely.
options.note: God redeems and gathers them; the nations do not free them.

options.text: He does not gather them.
options.note: He gathers them from Egypt, Assyria, and every land.

quiz.q: How is the cornerstone from Judah fulfilled?

options.text: In Christ, the chief cornerstone and sure foundation of God's people.
options.note: 1 Peter 2 and Psalm 118 present Christ as the cornerstone.

options.text: In a literal stone kept in the temple.
options.note: The cornerstone points to a person, ultimately Christ.

options.text: In the false shepherds God condemns.
options.note: The cornerstone replaces the false shepherds, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: It is never fulfilled.
options.note: It is fulfilled in Christ the chief cornerstone.

quiz.q: What does God intend for His restored people beyond bringing them home?

options.text: To strengthen them in the LORD so they walk in His name.
options.note: Verse 12, I will strengthen them, and in His name they will walk.

options.text: To leave them to fend for themselves.
options.note: He strengthens them rather than abandoning them.

options.text: To scatter them again immediately.
options.note: He gathers and strengthens them, not scatters them.

options.text: Nothing beyond a change of location.
options.note: He aims for a changed life walking in His name, not just relocation.

questions: The people sought guidance from idols that gave empty comfort. Where do you turn for direction that cannot really help, instead of asking the LORD?
God's anger burns against shepherds who exploit the flock. How does this shape the way you pray for and choose the leaders you follow?
Christ is the good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep. How does it comfort you to be cared for by Him rather than by failed leaders?
God gathers His people because He has redeemed them, not because they earned it. How does resting in His redemption change the way you approach Him?
God's goal is that His people walk in His name. What would it look like this week to walk in the LORD's name in your ordinary life?

translationNotes.note: Leaders can be rendered literally male goats, a figure for the ruling shepherds against whom God's anger burns before He attends to His flock.
Cornerstone, tent peg, and battle bow are images of firm support and strength emerging from Judah; Isaiah 22:23 uses the peg in a sure place for a faithful leader, pointing beyond human rulers.
Passing through the sea of distress and drying up the Nile evokes a new exodus, picturing God's gathering of His redeemed people home from every land.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Zechariah 10:1-12. All 12 verses reproduced in order. No em or en dashes present in this passage.
